questionText: Prove that for any prime number $p > 3$, the value $p^2 - 1$ is divisible by $24$.

steps.explanation: Recognising $p^2-1$ as a difference of squares turns a single number into a product of two factors that straddle $p$, which we can analyse individually.
To prove divisibility by $24$ it suffices to prove divisibility by $8$ and by $3$ separately, since $8$ and $3$ have no common factor.

steps.description: Use primality to fix the parity of p.
steps.explanation: The only even prime is $2$; every prime greater than $3$ is therefore odd. This controls the factors $p-1$ and $p+1$.

steps.workingLatex: p-1 \text{ and } p+1 \text{ are consecutive even integers}
steps.explanation: Since $p$ is odd, both $p-1$ and $p+1$ are even, and they differ by $2$, so they are consecutive even numbers.
Writing each even factor as twice an integer exposes two factors of $2$, and the halves $a,b$ turn out to be consecutive integers.
Multiplying the two even factors gives $4ab$; the factor $4$ is explicit, and we still need one more factor of $2$ to reach $8$.

steps.description: Use consecutiveness of a and b.
steps.explanation: Among two consecutive integers one is even, so their product $ab$ is even and can be written as $2c$.
Substituting $ab=2c$ turns $4ab$ into $8c$, so the product is divisible by $8$.

steps.description: Use primality to exclude a multiple of 3.
steps.explanation: A prime bigger than $3$ cannot be a multiple of $3$ (otherwise $3$ would divide it and it would not be prime), so $p \equiv 1$ or $2 \pmod 3$.
If $p$ leaves remainder $1$ modulo $3$, then $p-1$ is a multiple of $3$.
If $p$ leaves remainder $2$, then $p+1$ is a multiple of $3$. These are the only two possibilities, so one factor is always divisible by $3$.
In both admissible cases a factor is divisible by $3$, hence so is the product.
We have shown the product is divisible by both $8$ and $3$.
Because $8$ and $3$ are coprime, divisibility by each forces divisibility by their product $24$.
For every prime greater than $3$, $p^2-1$ is divisible by $24$, as required.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $p^2-1=(p-1)(p+1)$. As $p>3$ is prime it is odd, so $p-1,p+1$ are consecutive even numbers giving a factor of $8$; and $p\not\equiv0\pmod3$ forces $3$ to divide $p-1$ or $p+1$. Since $\gcd(8,3)=1$, $24\mid p^2-1$. $\blacksquare$
